agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"An existing production logistics system enforces a strict one-carrier-per-shipment invariant with supervisor-locked reassignment. The product team requests an auto-split feature that would automatically divide overweight shipments across multiple carriers without supervisor involvement.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of cargo to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport provider assigned to handle a Shipment."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The binding of exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Once set, only a Supervisor can change it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the only authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment that exceeds 500 kg."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed automatic process that divides an overweight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different Carrier, bypassing Supervisor approval."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a system integrity constraint — downstream processes (dispatch, tracking, billing, liability) all depend on a single Carrier being authoritative for a Shipment. Violating this creates data corruption and accountability gaps.","testable_assertion":"For any Shipment in the system, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization policy enforcing operational control. The constraint exists to prevent unauthorized changes, but a VP could adjust the policy (e.g., allow dispatchers to reassign under certain conditions).","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to change a Shipment's Carrier by a non-Supervisor role must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier assigned.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Dispatch without a single accountable Carrier breaks chain-of-custody, liability assignment, and physical handoff. This is a state integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if the Shipment does not have exactly one assigned Carrier."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Shipment weighing over 500 kg is created","when":"The auto-split feature processes it","then":"Each resulting sub-shipment must independently satisfy BR-1 (exactly one Carrier assigned per sub-shipment) and BR-3 (one Carrier at dispatch time)."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Shipment is split into sub-shipments","when":"Each sub-shipment receives a Carrier assignment","then":"That assignment must still be locked under Supervisor-only reassignment policy (BR-2), preserving the existing authorization model."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Shipment weighing over 500 kg is auto-split","when":"The original parent Shipment is examined","then":"Its lifecycle must be clearly defined — either it transitions to a terminal 'split' status and is no longer dispatchable, or the concept of parent-child Shipment relationships must be formally modeled."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A Shipment is auto-split into N sub-shipments","when":"Carrier assignment occurs on each sub-shipment","then":"No Supervisor involvement is required because these are NEW Carrier assignments on NEW sub-shipments, not reassignments on an existing Shipment."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment weighing exactly 500 kg — does it split or not?","expected_behavior":"Boundary must be explicitly defined: the threshold should be strictly greater than 500 kg to trigger a split, or >= 500 kg. The spec says 'exceeds 500 kg', implying strictly greater than."},{"scenario":"A Carrier is assigned to the original Shipment before the auto-split is triggered.","expected_behavior":"The existing Carrier assignment is locked by BR-2. Splitting the Shipment means the original assignment must be handled — either the original Carrier keeps one sub-shipment, or a Supervisor must release the lock before the split can proceed. This is the core tension."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment after splitting still exceeds 500 kg (e.g., a single indivisible item weighing 600 kg).","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting must have a termination condition. Indivisible items that exceed the threshold need an exception path."},{"scenario":"The auto-split occurs after a Carrier has already been assigned and the Shipment is en route to dispatch.","expected_behavior":"At what lifecycle stage can auto-split occur? If the Shipment already has a locked Carrier assignment, splitting it without Supervisor involvement directly contradicts BR-2."},{"scenario":"Tracking and billing: customer looks up their original Shipment ID.","expected_behavior":"The parent-child relationship must be visible. The customer should be able to track all sub-shipments from the original Shipment reference."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Auto-splitting enables the system to handle heavier shipments without manual intervention, potentially increasing throughput and revenue. However, multi-carrier shipments increase coordination costs and complexity in billing reconciliation.","operational":"Introduces significant operational complexity: multiple carriers must coordinate for what the customer sees as one shipment. Liability boundaries between carriers on a split shipment must be clearly defined. Failure of one sub-shipment affects the entire delivery.","user":"Customers benefit from seamless handling of heavy shipments. However, they may be confused by receiving multiple deliveries for a single order, or by multiple tracking numbers.","risk":"HIGH — the feature as stated directly contradicts two established production invariants. Implementing it without resolving the contradiction risks corrupting the domain model that downstream systems (dispatch, billing, tracking, liability) depend on.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over Carrier reassignment is bypassed. If auto-split is treated as 'new assignment' on new sub-shipments, their role is preserved; if treated as reassignment of the original, their authority is violated."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Multiple carriers now share responsibility for what was a single shipment. Liability boundaries, SLAs, and compensation models must be defined."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"A single shipment becomes multiple deliveries. Tracking, communication, and delivery experience are affected."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Billing model must handle split shipments — pro-rated charges, multi-carrier invoicing, dispute resolution across carriers."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"The core domain model must be extended with parent-child Shipment relationships, a new Shipment lifecycle state, and the auto-split process — all without breaking existing invariants."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction between the existing production invariants (one Carrier per Shipment, Supervisor-only reassignment) and the proposed feature (multiple carriers, no Supervisor involvement). The feature CAN be made compatible with the domain model, but only if 'sub-shipments' are treated as first-class Shipments that each independently satisfy all existing invariants — and this reframing is not stated in the feature request. As written, the request contradicts the established model.","high_confidence_areas":["The one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant is clearly established and non-negotiable","The Supervisor-only reassignment policy is explicitly stated","The auto-split feature as literally described conflicts with both"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be independent Shipments (which would resolve the contradiction) or modifications to the original Shipment","At what lifecycle stage auto-split should occur (before or after initial Carrier assignment)","Whether the 500 kg threshold is configurable or fixed"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model is correctly described and actively enforced in production","Sub-shipment is a new concept not yet modeled in the system","The product team has not yet considered the invariant conflicts"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The existing domain model mandates exactly one Carrier per Shipment (BR-1, BR-3) and Supervisor-only reassignment (BR-2). The auto-split feature requests multiple carriers handling parts of a single shipment without Supervisor involvement. These requirements directly conflict. Resolution path: if sub-shipments are modeled as independent first-class Shipments, each with its own single Carrier assignment, the one-Carrier invariant is preserved. But the feature request does not frame it this way, and the Supervisor bypass still needs justification.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations / Supervisors","Engineering","Carriers"],"options":["Model sub-shipments as independent first-class Shipments that each satisfy all existing invariants. Auto-split creates new Shipments (not reassignments), so Supervisor involvement is not required. Original Shipment transitions to a terminal 'split' state.","Relax the one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant to allow multi-carrier Shipments. This is a fundamental domain model change with cascading impact on dispatch, billing, tracking, and liability.","Require Supervisor approval for the auto-split operation itself, preserving the authorization model while enabling the feature."],"recommendation":"Option 1: Model sub-shipments as independent Shipments. This preserves all existing invariants, avoids Supervisor bypass (because creating new Shipments is not reassignment), and contains the change to a new 'split' lifecycle concept rather than weakening core invariants."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-2 states only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier. The auto-split feature explicitly states 'without requiring supervisor involvement.' If the original Shipment already has a Carrier assigned, splitting it and assigning new Carriers to sub-shipments while altering or voiding the original assignment constitutes a change that the current model reserves for Supervisors only.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Engineering"],"options":["Restrict auto-split to only occur BEFORE initial Carrier assignment, avoiding any reassignment conflict.","Treat the split as a system-level operation distinct from reassignment, with its own authorization rules.","Require Supervisor approval for splits that affect an already-assigned Carrier."],"recommendation":"Restrict auto-split to pre-assignment stage, or formally define 'split' as a distinct domain operation (not a reassignment) with clear rules for how the original Carrier assignment is resolved."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The auto-split feature as literally described requires multiple carriers on a single shipment, violating the one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"The auto-split feature bypasses Supervisor authority for what effectively constitutes carrier reassignment when an existing assignment is altered by the split.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."}]}
